title: podman play compose -f docker-compose.yml
body:
/kind feature

Use `docker-compose.yml`/`container-compose.yml` directly. Because setting up `podman` is to work with `docker-compose` is not user friendly.

**Describe the results you expected:**

```
podman play compose -f docker-compose.yml
```

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

The most straightforward approach is to use https://kompose.io/ as a lib to do conversion into Kubernetes YAML on the fly and then invoke `podman play kube` internally.

comments.body:
I would prefer that the first approach is to use docker-compose CLI, and be expected to export the right `DOCKER_HOST` env for it to work

There are many items that rely on `docker-compose` being just that, the CLI, instead of being called from podman. For example, many test suites that support docker-compose integration are built around executing commands via that CLI

Conforming podman to run `special sauce` around `podman play compose` would result in pain when dealing with this. I'd end up having to write aliases in test env's for;
```
alias docker-compose="podman play compose"
```

Which may not even work for some of the more advanced APIs out there

`docker-compose` is a development tool - for `production` you still need something like Kubernetes to split containers over multiple hosts, so my use story is not only about making it easier to run `docker-compose.yml` files, but also to make it easier to compare the environments.

As for test suites that rely on `docker-compose` CLI I really haven't seen any, and is interested to see some examples. For testing I would use the infra config that is as close to production as possible.

I did just say that it's used heavily in test suites, implying not production 😀 

Prime example - https://github.com/avast/gradle-docker-compose-plugin

For use in running integration tests on gradle

It's almost impossible for most companies above a certain scale to use infra in testing that's similar to production

We're talking integration tests. I.e either replicated locally, or in some CI pipeline. That will never be the same as prod or even stage
I have downloaded the docker-compose binary and run the system socket using `podman system service -t 0 unix:///var/run/docker.sock` and it works for me very okay better than expected.
@shyim that's a rootful `podman`.
@baude @Luap99 PTAL
@abitrolly works also for rootless https://wiki.archlinux.org/title/Podman#Docker_Compose
@shyim and still root is required to start `podman.service`.
@abitrolly  You can use the podman service as rootless. `systemctl --user start podman.socket` will create the socket at `$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/podman/podman.sock`

@Luap99 confirming it works this way.
```
$ systemctl --user start podman.socket
$ docker-compose ps                   
Name   Command   State   Ports
------------------------------
```
Still not user friendly, unless `docker-compose` in Fedora is packaged to start the socket itself.

We don't want to have services automatically running, since this potentially could cause breakage.  Running rootful docker-compose against docker.service, would fail the same way if the docker.service was not enabled.

@rhatdan what is difference between breakage caused by user executing `systemctl --user start podman.socket` to start `docker-compose` and the breakage caused by `docker-compose` calling `systemctl --user start podman.socket` to do what the user wants?
We have no control over docker-compose, you are welcome to open a PR on docker-compose to support that, but I don't think it is likely.

I actually thought that distributions can patch `docker-compose` to provide seamless user experience with `podman`. But it needs to be clarified what could be broken by running `systemctl --user start podman.socket`.
Sure we might be able to do this for Fedora, but we don't ship docker-compose for RHEL and I am not sure if other distros would go along with the change.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

I am really not sure where we go with this.  I don't see Podman able to do anything with docker-compose package. 
If a user wanted to install docker-compose they have to setup a server to answer the requests on Fedora there are two servers that and work with docker-compose.  moby-engine and `podman service`.
podman service can also be run in rootless mode. We could eventually add podman compose/podman play compose, especially where docker is adding a docker compose command.

But this would not force the service to be available.  Administrators/users still need to enable the service.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
So. no [`docker compose`](https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/commandline/compose/) support in `podman`?

Why do you say that?  We obviously support most Docker-compose work loads.  If you want to troll and say that the latest version does not work well, then fine. If you want to see this happen faster, then roll up your sleeves and start contributing.

> Why do you say that?
```
✗ podman compose 
Error: unrecognized command `podman compose`
```

And then you're saying that "Administrators/users still need to enable the service.", but neither `docker compose` nor `podman play` require any services to be enabled, and you've closed the issue, so from my side it looks like you are not going to implement the same UX.

Podman compose does not exist in the podman command (podman-compose does, but this is a different tool).

Docker compose requires the docker daemon or podman service to be running.  So Docker and Podman have the same requirements.

Podman play kube works without a daemon.  Docker-compose does not.
https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/commandline/compose/ docs doesn't mention that `docker compose` requires any daemons to be manually managed by users or admins.
Try running it on a system with docker daemon shut down.
But the docker daemon is enabled and started be default when installed. So why `podman` couldn't improve on that by bringing the API on demand?

The docker daemon is not installed and started by default on any distribution I know of?  Or are you talking about on a MAC?

Fedora, RHEL, Centos, Ubuntu, Debian ... Do not start any services that get installed by default.  The administrator has to enable to service after installation.
Okay. I may be wrong. Haven't used Docker in a while.
No problem.
